ground_truth.title: How Do I Get into Event Planning? 5 Ways to Kickstart Your Events Career
ground_truth.author: Social Tables
ground_truth.main_content: Just as there isn't one route to a great event, there isn't a single tried and true path that magically leads to a career in event planning. This is a field that thrives on ingenuity, creativity, and constant reinvention. Still, there are smart steps to take to begin the journey, build skills, and make a name for yourself. Read on for a roundup of the top strategies for kickstarting a successful and rewarding event planning career. Section 1 covers building a strong foundation of industry knowledge. Education is a great way to gain foundational knowledge of the events industry and the overall hospitality industry. Differentiate yourself from other job seekers with an industry-specific educational foundation. The events industry is growing, and demand for event planners is expected to grow at a faster-than-average rate of seven percent through 2028. You don't need a degree to become an event planner, but certain qualifications and certificates can help you get noticed and hired. There are dozens of colleges and universities that offer degrees in related fields, stand-alone event courses, meetings designations, and certificate programs. An event planner should be organized, open to collaboration, willing to adapt, creative, focused, able to multitask, good with budgeting, an excellent communicator, composed and calm under pressure, a networking maven, ready to negotiate, prepared to travel. Section 2 discusses parlaying all event and non-event experience into relevant experience. Cultivating a diversified experience portfolio, within the events industry or not, shows you developed a varied and valuable skill set. Do you have food and beverage experience? Your relationship-building skills, food and nutrition knowledge, and multitasking abilities are all strengths. Did you work in retail? Use your people skills and customer insights to design event experiences attendees love. For building an event planning portfolio as an events newbie, put your creativity on display with staged event photos, tailor your online presence with focused platforms, embrace different portfolio formats, roll up your sleeves for a good cause with charitable organizations, and gather testimonials from clients. Section 3 addresses balancing logistical experience and creative output. An outstanding event professional engages their logical side and their creative side in their work. Logic and logistics are the foundation of successful events. You must be interested in the nitty-gritty details, not just the creative big picture. You'll be pondering event budgets and floor plan layouts, which require the ability to think and plan logically. Section 4 covers planting seeds for an event planning career in a sector you love. Many types of organizations hold events and are looking to hire professionals that can take on a planning role. To grasp the varied opportunities of the events industry, keep these two acronyms in mind: MEEC stands for Meetings, Expositions, Events, and Conventions markets, which include travel, hospitality, and corporate events. SMERF stands for Social, Military, Educational, Religious, and Fraternal markets, which include weddings, birthday parties, and family reunions. Section 5 focuses on making extensive connections in the events industry. The number one way to kickstart your career in events is to network, network, network. It is all about who you know and who knows you. To put yourself out there, attend networking events hosted by hospitality organizations. For in-person networking, learn how to join a conversation by watching for people with stances that are angled slightly open, commit to starting one or two conversations, develop an open-minded curious mindset, remember proper business card etiquette, and follow up within 24 hours. For online networking, know who to contact, personalize your outreach, and follow up. How do event planners get their first client? For many planners, the first client is a friend of the family who needs a creative planner on a budget. You need to capture interest beyond your immediate network to build a client base and expand your skills. Share your portfolio with your networking contacts and clarify that you are open to referrals. There is no sure-fire track to an event planning career, but these five strategies will give you a good jump on the journey.
with: Just as there isn't one route to a great event, there isn't a single tried and true path that magically leads to a career in event planning.
The events industry is growing and demand for event planners is expected to grow at a faster-than-average rate of seven percent through 2028.
An event planner should be organized, open to collaboration, willing to adapt, creative, focused, able to multitask, good with budgeting, an excellent communicator, composed and calm under pressure.
The number one way to kickstart your career in events is to network, network, network—it is all about who you know and who knows you.
There is no sure-fire track to an event planning career, but these five strategies will give you a good jump on the journey.
